teaching_contract.concrete_problem: A model completion cannot be debugged or trusted when the harness loses the exact messages sent, returned text, and provider evidence.
teaching_contract.first_principle: The model returns data; the harness owns the boundary that records and normalizes that data before the rest of the system uses it.
worked_walkthrough: Follow one FakeModel call from the supplied message list to a provider response.
Trace how call_model turns the response into one ModelCallRecord instead of exposing provider-specific data throughout the system.

boundary_and_invariant.invariant: Every successful call preserves the exact input messages, returns assistant text as a string, and retains inspectable evidence.

options: Coerce arbitrary provider output into text so the loop continues.
Reject a non-string completion at the provider boundary.
design_tension.decision_rule: Reject data that violates the internal text contract; preserving an explicit failure is safer than hiding a provider-shape change.

teaching_contract.prediction_prompt: Before opening the implementation, predict what call_model should do when FakeModel returns a non-string completion and explain why.
teaching_contract.artifact_inspection_prompt: Inspect the starter types and failing FakeModel test; identify which facts exist before call_model is implemented and which record fields the test can prove.

build: Implement the narrow call_model normalization and ModelCallRecord creation path.
leave_unchanged: Do not add tool dispatch, retries, or message-state policy to this primitive.

proof_interpretation.establishes: The focused tests establish the input, output-type, and evidence-record contract for the fake provider cases.
proof_interpretation.does_not_establish: They do not establish that a real provider is reliable, that tools execute safely, or that a multi-step agent can recover.

teaching_contract.transfer_prompt: If a second provider returns a rich response object, decide whether its metadata belongs in the record, the normalized text contract, or a provider adapter.

study_contract.objective: Leave with a plain-language model of the provider boundary, a narrow call_model change, and a testable claim about its record.

jot_notes.label: Jot notes
jot_notes.placeholder: Raw provider shapes, record fields, test output, and questions. Keep this messy.

prompts.prompt: Explain the provider boundary in one sentence.

prompts.label: What must it protect?
prompts.prompt: Name the output contract call_model must enforce.

prompts.label: What would prove it?
prompts.prompt: Name the focused test assertion or uncertainty you still need to inspect.

plan.intro: Plan only the provider-normalization slice; do not grow an agent loop.

smallest_slice.placeholder: Reject a non-string completion while preserving the record.

must_do.placeholder: Exact messages, string output, and evidence fields.

open_question.placeholder: Which provider metadata belongs in the record?

feynman.placeholder: Use plain language and one analogy.

feynman_limit.prompt: What provider-specific detail does the analogy hide?

prediction_vs_evidence.prompt: Compare your prediction with the exact proof output.

next_step.prompt: Name one focused implementation or inspection step.

0001-model-call-primitive.learning_goal: Preserve an inspectable model-call record while normalizing provider output at one boundary.
0001-model-call-primitive.learner_decision: Decide which provider response data belongs in ModelCallRecord and reject a non-string completion.

target_artifacts.expected_artifact: A ModelCallRecord with exact messages, string output, and evidence fields.

failure_contract.symptom: A provider response contains a non-string completion.

micro_world.rationale: One annotated request and response exposes the complete boundary.

teaching_contract.concrete_problem: An agent repeats read_file even after the tool succeeded because the next model call cannot see the observation.
teaching_contract.first_principle: An agent has no hidden durable memory between calls; its message sequence is the state handed back to the model.
worked_walkthrough: Compare the first model-call input with the second after read_file returns a result.
Follow the user task, assistant request, and tool observation in order to see why the second call can make a different decision.

boundary_and_invariant.boundary: Message-state accumulation owns the transition from an event to a correctly ordered next-call message.
boundary_and_invariant.invariant: Before a next model call, every relevant tool observation is appended after the request that caused it without changing prior message order.

options: Ask the model again and rely on it to remember the tool result.
Append a normalized tool observation to explicit message state before asking again.
design_tension.decision_rule: Make evidence explicit in the next input; the harness, not an assumed model memory, owns the state transition.

teaching_contract.prediction_prompt: When read_file succeeded but the model repeats the request, commit to the first evidence to inspect and explain how it distinguishes missing context from model behavior.
teaching_contract.artifact_inspection_prompt: Inspect ModelCallRecord from Primitive 1 and the message-state starter test to separate recorded provider evidence from the new state that must be accumulated.

build: Implement ordered conversion and appending of user, assistant, and tool messages.
leave_unchanged: Do not call the model again, reorder history, or decide whether a tool request is valid.

proof_interpretation.establishes: The second-call proof establishes that the model receives the user, assistant, and tool observation in the required order.
proof_interpretation.does_not_establish: It does not prove that every model will stop repeating a request or that the requested tool was safe to run.

teaching_contract.transfer_prompt: When a tool fails instead of succeeding, decide which observation should still be appended and why hiding that failure would make the next decision worse.

procedural.evidence: The learner reconstructs four message-state helpers from a real scaffold.

diagnostic.evidence: The learner identifies the first missing transition, responsible component, smallest correction, and regression evidence.

conditional.evidence: The learner distinguishes absent context from a model decision made with complete context.

design.evidence: The lesson compares assumed hidden memory with an explicit ordered message ledger.

evidence.evidence: The learner inspects captured model calls and runs seven focused tests.

reconstruction.evidence: A real message_state_skeleton.py withholds the current primitive's implementation.

transfer.evidence: The learner applies the same state reasoning to a failed tool observation.

study_contract.objective: Leave knowing that message history is agent state, with a narrow plan for appending the next observation in order.

jot_notes.label: Jot notes
jot_notes.placeholder: Message roles, call order, tool observations, and confusions. Keep this messy.

prompts.label: What is the agent remembering?
prompts.prompt: Explain why the next model call only knows the messages it receives.

prompts.label: Which transition owns the fix?
prompts.prompt: Name what must happen before the second model call.

prompts.label: What would prove the order?
prompts.prompt: Name the second-call evidence or uncertainty you need to resolve.

plan.intro: Plan one message-state transition, not model behavior or tool validation.

smallest_slice.label: Smallest behavior
smallest_slice.placeholder: Append the tool observation after its assistant request.

must_not_do.label: Must not do
must_not_do.placeholder: Call the model again before appending the observation.

open_question.label: One uncertainty
open_question.placeholder: How should a failed tool observation be represented?

feynman.label: Explain this to a smart 12-year-old
feynman.subject: why an agent needs a written memory of what its tools did
feynman.placeholder: Use plain language and one analogy.

feynman_limit.label: Where does that explanation break?
feynman_limit.prompt: Which message-shape detail does your explanation hide?

prediction_vs_evidence.label: What did the second call change or confirm?
prediction_vs_evidence.prompt: Compare your expected message order with model.calls[1].

mental_model.label: What changed in your mental model?
mental_model.prompt: Capture why repeated tool calls can be a missing-context problem.

next_step.label: Next smallest step
next_step.prompt: Name one message transition to implement or inspect.

0002-message-state-primitive.learning_goal: Preserve ordered messages so a tool observation reaches the next model call.
0002-message-state-primitive.learner_decision: Diagnose whether a repeated tool request is model behavior or missing message-state context.

target_artifacts.expected_artifact: A second model call that includes the prior tool observation.

failure_contract.symptom: The agent repeats read_file after it already succeeded.

micro_world.rationale: A short timeline helps expose accumulation, but a full simulation is unnecessary.

teaching_contract.concrete_problem: Primitive 2 preserves assistant output as text, but tool-looking text may be malformed JSON, a valid JSON value with the wrong outer shape, or a structured request whose tool-specific validity is still unknown.
teaching_contract.first_principle: Text becomes structured data only when an explicit parser establishes a small, testable claim; looking structured is not the same as being parsed.
worked_walkthrough: Follow one assistant string through complete-document JSON decoding and strict ToolRequest outer-shape validation.
Contrast malformed text, a decoded JSON string, and a request with path=123 to locate the first failed claim or the later boundary that still owns the decision.

boundary_and_invariant.boundary: The tool-request parser owns complete JSON decoding and ToolRequest outer-envelope checks.
boundary_and_invariant.invariant: It returns one ToolRequest, INVALID_JSON, or INVALID_TOOL_REQUEST_SHAPE without deciding registry membership, tool-specific argument validity, runtime availability, or execution.

options: Accept convenient function-like or prose-wrapped syntax from the model.
Accept only one complete JSON object with the required outer shape.
design_tension.decision_rule: Keep the protocol strict and the parser's guarantee small so later boundaries receive unambiguous structured data.

teaching_contract.prediction_prompt: For a decoded request whose read_file path is the JSON number 123, commit to whether Primitive 3 returns INVALID_JSON, INVALID_TOOL_REQUEST_SHAPE, or ToolRequest, and explain which boundary owns the remaining decision.
teaching_contract.artifact_inspection_prompt: Inspect Primitive 2's exact assistant-message output, the real parser scaffold, and the focused parser tests; identify where raw text becomes a JSON value and where that value becomes ToolRequest.

build: Reconstruct parse_json, validate_tool_request, and parse_tool_request from the real scaffold and keep their error ownership distinct.
leave_unchanged: Do not add a tool registry, tool-specific argument schemas, runtime handler lookup, retries, or prose extraction.

proof_interpretation.establishes: The ten parser tests distinguish complete-document decoding failure, outer-shape failure, and structured requests, including unknown tools and tool-specific argument types left for Primitive 4.
proof_interpretation.does_not_establish: They do not prove that a parsed tool exists, that its arguments match that tool, that a handler is available, or that execution is safe.

teaching_contract.transfer_prompt: For a proposed top-level request_id, decide whether the shared protocol envelope should reject it, adopt it, or place it elsewhere, then name the parser and validation proofs needed for that contract change.

procedural.evidence: The learner reconstructs the parser's three functions from a real scaffold.

diagnostic.evidence: The learner locates the first failed gate and maps each stable error code to its owning function.

conditional.evidence: Ten cases require distinguishing decoding, outer shape, and later validation.

design.evidence: The learner compares permissive extraction with one strict complete-document protocol.

evidence.evidence: The learner inspects predecessor output, scaffold, and tests, then runs the focused proof.

reconstruction.evidence: 03_parse_tool_request_skeleton.py exposes contracts while withholding all three parser bodies.

transfer.evidence: The learner applies envelope ownership to a proposed request_id field and names cross-boundary regressions.

study_contract.objective: Leave able to separate raw text from structured tool requests and state exactly what parsing does not prove.

jot_notes.label: Jot notes
jot_notes.placeholder: Raw outputs, JSON shapes, parser cases, and boundary questions. Keep this messy.

prompts.label: What changes at parsing?
prompts.prompt: Explain the difference between assistant text and ToolRequest.

prompts.label: Where is the first failure?
prompts.prompt: Name the parser condition that accepts or rejects the current case.

prompts.label: What remains unproven?
prompts.prompt: State one later decision parsing cannot make.

plan.intro: Plan the strict text-to-ToolRequest boundary only.

smallest_slice.placeholder: Reject one malformed or wrong-shape input.

open_question.placeholder: Which outer shapes count as a request?

feynman.placeholder: Use plain language and one analogy.

next_step.prompt: Name one parser case to implement or inspect.

0003-manual-tool-protocol.learning_goal: Turn one assistant string into a narrow ToolRequest while distinguishing decoding, outer-shape, and later validation claims.
0003-manual-tool-protocol.learner_decision: Locate the first failed parser gate without assigning registry or tool-specific argument failures to Primitive 3.

target_artifacts.expected_artifact: A structured ToolRequest or a parse/outer-shape error, without tool-registry or argument validation.

failure_contract.symptom: Assistant output is malformed JSON or not a ToolRequest-shaped object.

micro_world.rationale: Immediate input and output feedback is useful, but persistent world state is not.

teaching_contract.concrete_problem: ToolRequest(tool=read_file, args={path: 123}) is parsed correctly but is not safe or meaningful to execute.
teaching_contract.first_principle: A parsed request is only structured text; a validation boundary must establish the tool-specific contract before runtime execution.
worked_walkthrough: Start with Primitive 3's ToolRequest to identify exactly what parsing already guarantees.
Walk the path type failure through registry lookup and argument validation before a runtime handler is even considered.

boundary_and_invariant.boundary: Tool argument validation owns protocol registry lookup and the registered tool's argument contract.
boundary_and_invariant.invariant: Only a request for a known tool with arguments matching its declared schema can leave validation as executable input.

options: Let read_file discover malformed arguments during execution.
Reject malformed or unknown requests before runtime dispatch using the protocol contract.
design_tension.decision_rule: Validate at the trust boundary, while keeping runtime-handler availability a distinct later decision rather than pretending one registry answers both questions.

teaching_contract.prediction_prompt: Before opening the validator, decide which boundary owns path=123, where known tools are declared, and whether runtime handler absence is the same failure.
teaching_contract.artifact_inspection_prompt: Inspect ToolRequest and the parser cases from Primitive 3; state what those artifacts prove and the new responsibility they deliberately leave open.

build: Implement known-tool and argument-shape validation with explicit result codes.
leave_unchanged: Do not execute read_file or merge protocol registry ownership with runtime handler availability.

proof_interpretation.establishes: The validation cases establish the declared protocol's behavior for known tools and their arguments.
proof_interpretation.does_not_establish: They do not establish filesystem permission, handler registration, or successful tool execution.

teaching_contract.transfer_prompt: For submit, decide whether protocol-valid but non-executable control should pass validation, fail dispatch, or terminate before dispatch, and name the evidence needed to decide.

study_contract.objective: Leave able to distinguish structured requests from executable requests and plan a narrow validation boundary.

jot_notes.label: Jot notes
jot_notes.placeholder: ToolRequest facts, schemas, registry decisions, and unresolved ownership. Keep this messy.

prompts.label: What does parsing leave open?
prompts.prompt: Explain why path=123 can be structured yet not executable.

prompts.label: Which boundary owns it?
prompts.prompt: Choose the owner of known-tool and argument checks.

prompts.label: What must stay separate?
prompts.prompt: Name one question validation cannot answer about runtime execution.

plan.intro: Plan protocol validation without silently moving runtime-dispatch responsibilities into it.

must_do.label: Must decide
must_do.placeholder: Known tool and argument schema validity.

must_not_do.label: Must not decide
must_not_do.placeholder: Filesystem permission or runtime handler availability.

open_question.label: One uncertainty
open_question.placeholder: How should submit interact with later dispatch?

feynman.label: Explain this to a smart 12-year-old
feynman.subject: why a correctly shaped request is not automatically safe to execute
feynman.placeholder: Use plain language and one analogy.

feynman_limit.label: Where does that explanation break?
feynman_limit.prompt: Which registry-versus-handler detail does the analogy hide?

prediction_vs_evidence.label: What did validation evidence change or confirm?
prediction_vs_evidence.prompt: Compare your chosen owner with the test result.

mental_model.label: What changed in your mental model?
mental_model.prompt: Capture the difference between protocol validity and runtime availability.

next_step.label: Next smallest step
next_step.prompt: Name one validation case to implement or inspect.

0004-schema-validation.learning_goal: Validate registered tool arguments at the trust boundary before execution.
0004-schema-validation.learner_decision: Consume Primitive 3's ToolRequest and assign protocol registry, argument schema, and runtime-handler availability to their responsible source of truth.

target_artifacts.expected_artifact: Validation that accepts a Primitive 3 ToolRequest only when its tool and arguments are executable by the protocol contract.

failure_contract.symptom: A parsed read_file request has path=123.

micro_world.rationale: Validation outcomes are immediate and do not require an evolving simulated world.

teaching_contract.concrete_problem: A protocol-valid read_file request can still traverse outside the configured sandbox or produce a confusing file-system result.
teaching_contract.first_principle: Validation of an argument's shape is not authorization of the resource it names; the capability boundary must enforce containment at use time.
worked_walkthrough: Follow a relative nested path and a parent-traversal path through sandbox resolution.
Compare FORBIDDEN_PATH with missing-file and directory results to see why one generic failure would destroy useful causal evidence.

boundary_and_invariant.boundary: The sandboxed file-tool boundary owns canonical path containment and file-operation result normalization.
boundary_and_invariant.invariant: No read or write resolves outside the configured root, while allowed paths retain accurate success or failure observations.

options: Trust validated relative-looking paths and pass them directly to filesystem operations.
Resolve paths against the sandbox and reject any result that escapes it.
design_tension.decision_rule: Authorize the resolved capability, not a string that merely appears harmless before normalization.

teaching_contract.prediction_prompt: For each path case, predict whether it is contained, which result code applies, and why argument validation alone cannot decide it.
teaching_contract.artifact_inspection_prompt: Inspect Primitive 4's accepted request shape and the real file-tool cases; distinguish a protocol-valid request from an authorized filesystem operation.

build: Implement or extend contained read/write behavior and add one regression case tied to a rejected path.
leave_unchanged: Do not change parser syntax rules or redefine the protocol registry while fixing containment.

proof_interpretation.establishes: The focused file-tool proof establishes containment and normalized outcomes for its covered path and operation cases.
proof_interpretation.does_not_establish: It does not prove that every filesystem race, symlink policy, or future tool capability is safe.

teaching_contract.transfer_prompt: If a future tool accepts a URL rather than a path, decide which part of the containment reasoning transfers and which new authority boundary it needs.

study_contract.objective: Leave able to explain why valid arguments still need capability containment and plan one real sandbox regression.

jot_notes.label: Jot notes
jot_notes.placeholder: Paths, result codes, containment sketches, and edge cases. Keep this messy.

prompts.label: What does containment protect?
prompts.prompt: Explain why a relative-looking path still needs authorization after validation.

prompts.label: Which result belongs here?
prompts.prompt: Choose the contained or rejected outcome for the current path case.

prompts.label: What would prove it?
prompts.prompt: Name the path case and result code that would establish containment.

plan.intro: Plan one containment behavior and its regression; leave parser and registry rules alone.

must_do.label: Must preserve
must_do.placeholder: Allowed nested paths and accurate normalized results.

must_not_do.label: Must not change
must_not_do.placeholder: Parser syntax or tool registry policy.

open_question.label: One uncertainty
open_question.placeholder: Which filesystem case needs another result code?

feynman.label: Explain this to a smart 12-year-old
feynman.subject: why a tool must stay inside its allowed folder
feynman.placeholder: Use plain language and one analogy.

feynman_limit.label: Where does that explanation break?
feynman_limit.prompt: Which path-resolution detail does the analogy hide?

prediction_vs_evidence.label: What did the path proof change or confirm?
prediction_vs_evidence.prompt: Compare your expected result code with the test output.

mental_model.label: What changed in your mental model?
mental_model.prompt: Capture why argument validation is not filesystem authorization.

next_step.label: Next smallest step
next_step.prompt: Name one containment case to implement, inspect, or regress.

0005-sandboxed-file-tools.learning_goal: Enforce filesystem containment while returning useful file-tool observations.
0005-sandboxed-file-tools.learner_decision: Decide which path failures the tool boundary must reject and why validation alone is insufficient.

target_artifacts.expected_artifact: File tools that reject forbidden paths with FORBIDDEN_PATH.

failure_contract.symptom: A request traverses out of the configured sandbox.

micro_world.rationale: Spatial containment can benefit from manipulation, but existing path cases may teach it sufficiently.

teaching_contract.system_problem: A protocol-valid read_file request can reach the agent loop while the runtime tools dictionary has no read_file handler; the harness still has to preserve a bounded, explainable trajectory.
teaching_contract.integration_responsibility: The loop coordinates already-built boundaries in a defined order, turns recoverable failures into observations, maintains message state, and stops safely.

existing_components.already_guarantees: Raw assistant text becomes either a structured request or a normalized parse failure.
existing_components.does_not_guarantee: The requested tool exists or can execute.

existing_components.already_guarantees: A known protocol tool has arguments that match its registered schema.
existing_components.does_not_guarantee: The runtime tools dictionary has a handler for that tool.

existing_components.already_guarantees: A permitted file operation returns a normalized result while containing paths.
existing_components.does_not_guarantee: The loop invoked it in the right order or will stop safely.

components.output_or_transition: Validated request or normalized rejection observation
components.responsibility: Establish the protocol contract before runtime dispatch.

components.input: Validated non-submit request and runtime handler map
components.output_or_transition: Tool result, missing-handler observation, or normalized tool exception
components.responsibility: Decide whether an executable handler is actually available.

components.input: User task, messages, model output, and each boundary result
components.output_or_transition: Next ordered messages or a submitted/max-steps AgentResult
components.responsibility: Own transition order, observation accumulation, and bounded stopping.

system_model.system_invariant: Every loop iteration records assistant output, invokes boundaries in order, appends a normalized observation for non-terminal outcomes, and never exceeds max_steps.

success_path: The model requests read_file with valid JSON and arguments.
The registered runtime handler returns a result that is appended as a tool observation before the next call, which submits the final answer.
failure_or_edge_path: The protocol registry accepts read_file, but runtime dispatch finds no read_file handler.
The loop appends UNKNOWN_TOOL as an observation, gives the model another bounded turn, and eventually returns submit or max_steps.
worked_trajectories.comparison_question: What evidence distinguishes an unknown protocol tool from a protocol-valid tool whose runtime handler is missing?

options: Abort the entire run whenever any component rejects a request.
Normalize recoverable parser, validation, and dispatch failures into observations while enforcing a bounded step budget.
design_tension.decision_rule: The loop preserves recoverable evidence for the next model decision, but it owns explicit terminal control flow for submit and max_steps.

teaching_contract.prediction_prompt: Before opening run_agent, predict the next message and responsible boundary when read_file validates successfully but the runtime tools dictionary is empty.
teaching_contract.artifact_inspection_prompt: Inspect the parser, validator, file-tool contracts, agent-loop tests, and causal scenario to map what each component proves before the loop composes them.

intervention_strategy.scaffold: The real agent_loop.py reconstruction handoff, with the control-flow slice intentionally withheld from copying.
build_order: Append assistant output to message state.
Validate the output and append a normalized rejection observation when validation fails.
Return immediately for submit control flow.
Look up and invoke a runtime handler only after validation.
Normalize missing handlers and tool exceptions into observations.
Stop after the configured max_steps budget.
learner_owns: The order of loop transitions and message-state updates.
At least one normalized runtime failure path and its regression evidence.
leave_unchanged: Parser, validator, and file-tool internals unless diagnosis identifies them as the first bad boundary.
forbidden_shortcuts: Treating a tool exception as invisible control flow.

required_evidence: A normalized missing-runtime-handler or tool-exception trajectory.
integration_proof.establishes: The focused suite establishes the tested ordering, failure normalization, dispatch, submit, and bounded-stop behavior of this composed harness.
integration_proof.does_not_establish: It does not establish reliable model behavior, correctness of every future tool, production observability, or broad behavioral evaluation coverage.

teaching_contract.causal_explanation_prompt: Explain one real failed trajectory as component transitions and contracts; do not attribute the failure merely to the model behaving strangely.
teaching_contract.transfer_prompt: Change one condition—malformed output, invalid arguments, a missing runtime handler, a tool exception, or exhausted budget—and name the first bad transition, smallest safe intervention, and regression proof.

study_contract.objective: Leave able to map an agent trajectory across boundaries, reconstruct one narrow loop slice, and explain proof evidence without blaming the model by default.

jot_notes.label: Jot notes
jot_notes.placeholder: Transitions, component responsibilities, trace clues, and unresolved causes. Keep this messy.

prompts.label: What owns each transition?
prompts.prompt: Map parser, validator, dispatch, and loop responsibilities in your own words.

prompts.label: What happens next?
prompts.prompt: Predict the next message when a protocol-valid tool has no runtime handler.

prompts.label: What would distinguish the cause?
prompts.prompt: Name the trace or test evidence that separates protocol failure from runtime drift.

plan.intro: Plan one ordered loop transition or failure-normalization slice; do not rewrite every prerequisite component.

smallest_slice.label: Smallest transition
smallest_slice.placeholder: Normalize a missing runtime handler into an observation.

must_do.label: Must preserve
must_do.placeholder: Boundary order, message accumulation, submit, and max_steps.

must_not_do.label: Must not bypass
must_not_do.placeholder: Validation, runtime-handler lookup, or bounded stopping.

open_question.label: One uncertainty
open_question.placeholder: Which failures should remain recoverable observations?

feynman.label: Explain this to a smart 12-year-old
feynman.subject: why a model requests actions but the harness owns every step
feynman.placeholder: Use plain language and one analogy.

feynman_limit.label: Where does that explanation break?
feynman_limit.prompt: Which parser, validation, or runtime distinction does the analogy hide?

prediction_vs_evidence.label: What did the trajectory change or confirm?
prediction_vs_evidence.prompt: Compare your predicted next transition with the trajectory test or trace output.

mental_model.label: What changed in your mental model?
mental_model.prompt: Capture how component contracts compose into loop behavior.

next_step.label: Next smallest step
next_step.prompt: Name one reconstruction or causal-explanation step.

0006-agent-loop-primitive.learning_goal: Predict and explain how the harness advances, rejects, executes, recovers, and stops.
0006-agent-loop-primitive.learner_decision: Diagnose the first bad transition and choose the smallest safe correction across parser, validator, runtime registry, and loop control.

target_artifacts.expected_artifact: A bounded loop that normalizes failures and preserves message-state observations.

failure_contract.symptom: A protocol-valid tool can be absent from the runtime handlers.

micro_world.rationale: Harness decisions evolve through hidden, branching state and are best learned causally.

incident.symptom: A completed run reports final_answer=null and exit_reason=max_steps, but contains no causal fields explaining how it reached that result.
available_evidence: The existing run_agent control flow and its focused tests.
incident.impact: The learner cannot decide whether the model repeated work, parsing failed, validation rejected a request, runtime dispatch failed, a tool raised, or the budget was exhausted for a legitimate trajectory.

diagnostic_model.first_principle: A symptom is not a root cause; the earliest violated contract must be supported by recorded evidence, and missing evidence is itself a valid diagnosis.

candidate_causes.cause: The model repeatedly requested a tool until max_steps.
candidate_causes.would_explain: A max-steps result with no final answer.
candidate_causes.distinguishing_evidence: Assistant output and ordered step records.

candidate_causes.cause: A parser, validator, or runtime boundary rejected work before it could complete.
candidate_causes.would_explain: A max-steps result after repeated normalized observations.
candidate_causes.distinguishing_evidence: Per-step parse, validation, runtime-handler, and tool-result fields.

candidate_causes.cause: The run has no causal record at all.
candidate_causes.would_explain: Why the supplied final result cannot distinguish either prior cause.
candidate_causes.distinguishing_evidence: A trace schema produced at the loop boundaries where each fact becomes authoritative.

diagnostic_model.ownership_rule: When existing output cannot distinguish plausible first bad transitions, the owning intervention is the boundary that first knows the missing fact—not an invented guess about model behavior.

observations: partial-run.json contains only final_answer and exit_reason.
The current agent loop already knows assistant output, validation results, runtime handler availability, tool results, and stopping decisions while it executes.
elimination_steps: The final result cannot rule in malformed output, an unavailable handler, a tool exception, or repeated valid work.
Because no recorded artifact distinguishes those causes, claiming one would be speculation rather than diagnosis.
worked_investigation.current_conclusion: The incident is evidence-insufficient: causal loop transitions must be recorded before this run can be explained.
worked_investigation.confidence_limit: The partial fixture proves neither that the model was at fault nor which loop boundary first changed the trajectory.

teaching_contract.prediction_prompt: Before opening the logger starter, choose the first missing causal field needed to distinguish a rejected request from a repeated valid request, and explain which loop boundary owns it.

artifact_inspection_sequence.question: Which concrete diagnostic questions can this output answer?
artifact_inspection_sequence.fact_established: It records the terminal outcome but no causal transition.

artifact_inspection_sequence.question: Where do assistant output, validation, dispatch, tool result, and exit reason first become authoritative?
artifact_inspection_sequence.fact_established: The loop has the required facts at ordered decision boundaries.

artifact_inspection_sequence.question: Which fields must one replayable trace preserve?
artifact_inspection_sequence.fact_established: The intended proof requires initial messages, causal steps, and terminal outcome.

diagnosis_commitment.ambiguity_rule: The learner must conclude evidence is insufficient when the available artifacts cannot distinguish the plausible causes; confidence is not a substitute for a trace.

intervention_strategy.smallest_safe_intervention: Implement TraceLogger and add only the hooks needed for run_agent to capture initial messages, one causal record per step, and the terminal result without changing loop behavior.
leave_unchanged: Parser, validator, runtime dispatch, tool behavior, and max_steps policy unless the new trace actually identifies one as responsible.
forbidden_shortcuts: Synthesizing a trace after the run from the final result.
Logging only final_answer and calling it diagnosis.
Changing loop control flow solely to make output easier to explain.

required_evidence: A real agent-loop trajectory that produces a structured JSON trace.
The trace test that asserts each required causal field and replayable serialization.
diagnostic_proof.establishes: The covered run records the fields needed to distinguish its assistant output, validation, runtime dispatch, tool outcome, and terminal decision.
diagnostic_proof.does_not_establish: The trace does not prove the model chose the best action, capture every external cause, or make an untested trajectory diagnosable.

teaching_contract.causal_explanation_prompt: Use one produced trace to explain the incident as evidence gap or first bad transition → owning boundary → intervention → regression, without defaulting to the model as the cause.
teaching_contract.transfer_prompt: Given a trace that records validation but omits runtime-handler and tool-result fields, decide which hypotheses remain ambiguous, the smallest additional event needed, and the regression proof you would add.

study_contract.objective: Leave able to separate an observed outcome from an explained cause, add the smallest causal trace, and use the resulting evidence without blaming the model by default.

jot_notes.label: Jot notes
jot_notes.placeholder: Facts, candidate causes, missing fields, boundary owners, and questions. Keep this messy.

prompts.label: What can this run actually tell you?
prompts.prompt: From partial-run.json alone, separate the facts you can state from causes you cannot yet claim.

prompts.label: What is the next useful evidence?
prompts.prompt: Choose the first causal field that would distinguish a rejected request from repeated valid work, and name the loop boundary that first knows it.

prompts.label: Which tempting diagnosis must wait?
prompts.prompt: Name one plausible explanation that the partial result cannot support yet, and the trace field that could test it.

plan.intro: Plan the smallest instrumentation slice that turns one future trajectory into causal evidence; do not repair the loop speculatively.

target_function.label: Artifact to build or change
target_function.placeholder: TraceLogger plus the narrow run_agent hooks that emit causal records.

smallest_slice.label: Smallest evidence slice
smallest_slice.placeholder: Record initial messages, one ordered step fact, and the terminal result.

must_do.label: Must preserve
must_do.placeholder: Existing loop behavior while recording authoritative causal fields and replayable JSON.

must_not_do.label: Must not do
must_not_do.placeholder: Infer a trace after the run, change control flow to fit a story, or claim a model cause without evidence.

open_question.label: One evidence question
open_question.placeholder: At which boundary does each trace field first become authoritative?

feynman.label: Explain this to a smart 12-year-old
feynman.subject: why a final result is not enough to explain how an agent run went wrong
feynman.placeholder: Use plain language and follow one decision from the loop into the trace.

feynman_limit.label: Where does that explanation break?
feynman_limit.prompt: Which difference between missing evidence, a parser rejection, validation failure, and runtime failure does your explanation hide?

prediction_vs_evidence.label: What did the new trace confirm or rule out?
prediction_vs_evidence.prompt: Compare the causal field you predicted with the produced trace and state which candidate cause it supports, weakens, or still leaves unresolved.

mental_model.label: What changed in your mental model?
mental_model.prompt: Capture how a loop boundary becomes responsible for evidence when it is the first place to know a fact.

next_step.label: Next smallest step
next_step.prompt: Name one trace field, regression assertion, or changed-symptom diagnosis to do next.

0007-trace-logger.learning_goal: Use a causal trace to separate model behavior from harness behavior.
0007-trace-logger.learner_decision: Design and implement the smallest causal trace that distinguishes model output, harness decisions, tool outcomes, and stop reason.

target_artifacts.expected_artifact: A replayable JSON trace with one causal record for every agent-loop decision.

failure_contract.symptom: A final answer without causal fields cannot diagnose the run.

micro_world.rationale: A real incomplete run makes the missing causal evidence directly visible.

teaching_contract.decision_question: Does the composed file-agent harness satisfy the explicitly claimed copy-file behavior across defined trajectories, rather than merely passing its focused component tests?

behavioral_claim.hypothesis: If each composed case is run against fixed model outputs, tools, and budget, then the eval runner can report whether the actual trajectory satisfies every expected behavior and preserve evidence for failures.
behavioral_claim.scope: The configured deterministic Project 1A eval cases and their declared expected outcomes; not arbitrary provider behavior or all future workflows.
expected_failure_modes: A trajectory reaches submit with the wrong final answer.
A required side effect such as target file content differs from the expected outcome.
A failed case loses its trace path or a specific failure reason.

existing_evidence: Focused Agent Loop tests show individual transitions such as tool-result accumulation and submit control flow.
The Trace Logger test shows one trajectory can preserve causal fields.
baseline.what_baseline_establishes: The prerequisite components work for their focused cases and can produce a causal trace.
baseline.what_baseline_does_not_establish: Focused tests do not grade a composed user workflow across a named case set or report per-case expected-versus-actual behavior.

measurement_model.unit_of_evaluation: One complete, named agent trajectory with a user task, fixed model outputs, runtime tools, expected final behavior, and trace path.

cases.source: curriculum/phase-1/08_eval_runner/fixtures/eval-cases.json plus the real wrong-answer case in tests_eval_runner.py.
cases.inclusion_rule: Each case must exercise a user-visible composed behavior and declare evidence sufficient to explain a mismatch.
minimum_coverage: A successful multi-tool workflow with an expected side effect.
A near-identical trajectory whose final answer or other expected field is wrong.

outcome_contract.pass_condition: Every declared expected field equals the actual result.
outcome_contract.failure_evidence: The per-case actual and expected records remain available for inspection.

outcome_contract.pass_condition: At least one declared expected field differs from actual behavior.
outcome_contract.failure_evidence: The report preserves a specific failure reason and causal trace path.

controlled_conditions: Use deterministic FakeModel outputs for each configured case.
Keep the tool implementation, max_steps budget, and expected case contract fixed while evaluating a case.
confounders: Changing model outputs, tools, budget, and expected behavior together would make a result impossible to attribute.
An aggregate pass count without per-case records can hide which behavior changed.

baseline_run: The copy-file trajectory reads a.txt, writes b.txt, and submits copied.
Focused component tests can show individual transitions but do not produce a named expected-versus-actual evaluation report.
measured_run: The same copy-file case is evaluated against its expected exit_reason, final_answer, and target_content.
A nearly identical run that submits wrong produces a failed EvalCaseResult with a failure reason and trace path.
worked_comparison.interpretation: The report supports a claim about only its configured cases; the wrong-answer result shows why a score without failed-case evidence is not enough for diagnosis.

teaching_contract.prediction_prompt: Before implementing the runner, predict whether the wrong-answer trajectory should count as passing because its tools succeeded, and identify the exact expected field that decides the result.
teaching_contract.artifact_inspection_prompt: Inspect the eval fixture, the copy-file trajectory, and the trace contract; distinguish what unit tests already prove from the composed behavior this experiment measures.

experiment_strategy.intervention_or_measurement_change: Implement one-case evaluation and suite aggregation so expected-versus-actual comparison, per-case failure reason, and trace path become repeatable evidence.
keep_constant: The configured case inputs and expected outcomes while the runner is constructed.
learner_owns: Comparison of every declared expected field.
Aggregate report counts derived from per-case results.
Failure reporting that retains causal trace linkage.
leave_unchanged: Agent-loop control flow, trace logger behavior, and the case's tool implementations unless a measured failure identifies them as responsible.
forbidden_shortcuts: Calling a single passing trajectory an evaluation.
Changing model outputs and expected outcomes together while claiming a behavioral improvement.
Reporting only an aggregate score without failed-case evidence.

required_evidence: A passing per-case result with expected and actual behavior.
A failing wrong-answer result with a specific failure reason and trace path.
Aggregate total, passed, and failed counts derived from those case results.
measurement_proof.establishes: The runner measures the declared deterministic cases, reports each outcome, and preserves the evidence needed to inspect a covered failure.
measurement_proof.does_not_establish: It does not establish production success rates, statistical significance, generalization to unlisted tasks, or that a passing aggregate score explains every failure mode.

teaching_contract.interpretation_prompt: State whether the result supports the configured behavioral claim, then connect one pass or failure to its expected-versus-actual record and causal trace path.
teaching_contract.transfer_prompt: When adding a new failure mode, decide which variables must stay fixed, whether it needs a new eval case, a unit regression, or both, and what evidence a failed result must preserve.

study_contract.objective: Leave able to turn a narrow behavioral claim into a controlled evaluation, interpret each case's evidence, and state the limits of the resulting report.

jot_notes.label: Jot notes
jot_notes.placeholder: Claims, cases, controlled variables, expected-versus-actual differences, and limits. Keep this messy.

prompts.label: What are we actually claiming?
prompts.prompt: State the narrow copy-file behavior this eval can support, then name one broader production claim it cannot support.

prompts.label: What must stay fixed?
prompts.prompt: Choose the model outputs, tools, budget, and expected behavior that must remain fixed before a changed result can be attributed to the runner or harness.

prompts.label: What makes a failed case useful?
prompts.prompt: Name the expected-versus-actual field, specific failure reason, and trace linkage needed to investigate a wrong-answer result.

plan.intro: Plan one controlled evaluation slice: compare a declared case with its actual trajectory and retain enough evidence to inspect a failure.

target_function.label: Artifact to build or change
target_function.placeholder: Eval runner case evaluation and report aggregation.

smallest_slice.label: Smallest measurement slice
smallest_slice.placeholder: Grade one deterministic case against expected fields and retain its actual record.

must_do.label: Must preserve
must_do.placeholder: Fixed case conditions, declared expected behavior, per-case evidence, and a causal trace path for failure.

must_not_do.label: Must not do
must_not_do.placeholder: Call one passing trajectory an evaluation, change inputs and expectations together, or collapse failures into only an aggregate score.

open_question.label: One measurement question
open_question.placeholder: Which expected field should decide a case whose tools all succeeded but final answer is wrong?

feynman.label: Explain this to a smart 12-year-old
feynman.subject: why one passing run is not yet evidence that an agent behavior works
feynman.placeholder: Use a simple comparison and explain why each case needs its own result.

feynman_limit.label: Where does that explanation break?
feynman_limit.prompt: Which difference between a unit assertion, a deterministic behavioral eval, and real production reliability does your explanation hide?

prediction_vs_evidence.label: What did the report confirm or weaken?
prediction_vs_evidence.prompt: Compare your predicted wrong-answer outcome with its per-case expected-versus-actual result, failure reason, and trace path.

mental_model.label: What changed in your mental model?
mental_model.prompt: Capture how controlled conditions and per-case evidence make a behavioral claim interpretable.

next_step.label: Next smallest step
next_step.prompt: Name one new case category, regression, or confounder check to add next.

0008-eval-runner.learning_goal: Evaluate multiple trajectories against explicit behavioral assertions.
0008-eval-runner.learner_decision: Implement a report that grades composed cases and preserves causal evidence for each failure.

target_artifacts.expected_artifact: A per-case eval report with expected and actual behavior, failure reason, and trace path.

failure_contract.symptom: Focused tests do not establish a composed user workflow.

micro_world.rationale: A real composed workflow fixture makes assertion coverage and aggregate outcomes concrete.
